title: Manufacturing Supply Chain (Feb 2023)
purpose: Understand where the manufacturing supply chain sits on evolution — what is industrialised versus still bespoke judgment

components.name: Distributor
components.rationale: Distributor channels are mature B2B relationships with established contracting practice; not yet commoditised (still relationship- and volume-negotiated).

components.name: Finished Product
components.rationale: The manufacturer's specific SKU has a defined market, known buyers, and productised features; industry-specific but inside the Product stage.

components.name: Compliance & Certification
components.rationale: ISO 9001, CE, UL, FDA 21 CFR and similar regimes are standardised frameworks with accredited certification bodies and audit-as-a-service vendors; clearly productised.

components.name: Inventory Strategy
components.rationale: As of Feb 2023, post-COVID and post-Ukraine shocks pushed JIT vs JIC into open contestation; firms are redoing this decision with bespoke stress-tests — no off-the-shelf answer, so Custom-Built with inertia (JIT-trained operators and existing supplier contracts resist change).

components.name: Sales Forecasting
components.rationale: Demand planning is served by a competitive vendor market (SAP IBP, Oracle Demantra, Kinaxis, o9, Anaplan) with published Gartner comparisons; firmly Product but with ML features pulling it right.

components.name: Assembly & Production
components.rationale: Industrial assembly is well understood; Lean/Six Sigma are codified; contract manufacturers (Foxconn, Flex, Jabil) sell it as a service. Still differentiated by industry vertical, so mid-Product.

components.name: Supply Chain Visibility
components.rationale: End-to-end SCV is an active investment area (project44, FourKites, Flexport, Interos) but full multi-tier integration is still bespoke per firm; most deployments are data-integration projects, not turnkey.

components.name: Freight
components.rationale: Global freight is utility-like: standardised containers, transparent spot rates, interchangeable carriers (Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM, FedEx, DHL). Invisible until it breaks — classic Commodity signal.

components.name: R&D
components.rationale: Product-development R&D is firm-specific by definition — it is the differentiation activity. Patterns exist (stage-gate, DFM) but the output is bespoke.

components.name: Equipment
components.rationale: Industrial machinery is a mature product market (Siemens, ABB, Mitsubishi, Fanuc, DMG Mori) with catalogued classes (CNCs, robots, presses) and published specs; leasing and finance are standard.

components.name: Warehousing
components.rationale: 3PL warehousing and fulfilment are utility-priced services; Amazon FBA, Prologis, GXO operate at commodity scale with per-pallet/per-sqft pricing.

components.name: Labour
components.rationale: Skilled industrial labour markets are well-understood; staffing agencies (Adecco, Randstad) productise recruitment; training pipelines are local but well-defined.

components.name: ROCE & Capital
components.rationale: ROCE is a textbook financial KPI; capital markets are utility-like; accounting standards (IFRS, GAAP) are fully codified.

components.name: Refined Materials
components.rationale: Refined inputs (steel sheet, plastic pellets, industrial chemicals) trade on catalogues and exchanges with standardised grades (ASTM, ISO); multiple interchangeable suppliers.

components.name: Lobbying
components.rationale: Firm-specific influence work — retained advisers, direct executive relationships, industry-association positioning. No productised 'lobbying-as-a-service' market for material regulatory outcomes; Genesis/early-Custom territory.

components.name: Raw Materials
components.rationale: Ores, oil, grains, cotton, base metals — exchange-traded globally (LME, CME, ICE). Textbook commodity.

annotations.text: JIT vs JIC: contested judgment call
